title: NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring
exclusions: Does not evaluate the quality of supplier security controls, interpret contractual clauses, approve vendor risk acceptance, determine Art.22 critical supply chain status, or replace procurement or board governance. Only correlates vulnerability findings, SBOM exposure, and vendor access patterns for critical suppliers.
evidence: Composite dashboard `UC-22.2.50`, Qualys export hash, `critical_suppliers.csv` revision ID, and weekly PDF archive row in `index=audit_evidence` referenced in the management body minutes.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(d) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(d) using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(2)(d).

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(3) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(3) using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(3).

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.22 receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.22 using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.22.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Seed Qualys events for two tier1 suppliers where open QID counts exceed the threshold and CVE severity maps to the high bucket; the saved search lists both supplier_name values with distinct asset counts and the auditor can open the drilldown to raw events.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Replay the same QID payloads after updating `critical_suppliers.csv` to mark those hosts as divested (`supplier_tier=exited`) so the lookup drops the rows and the saved search returns an empty statistic with a log entry confirming the suppressing lookup version.

dataSources: `index=vuln` `sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection` (HOSTNAME, QID, CVE_ID, Severity, STATUS) via Splunk Add-on for Qualys ([Splunkbase 2964](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2964)); `lookup=critical_suppliers.csv` (managed_asset, supplier_name, supplier_tier, jump_host); `index=sbom` `sourcetype=sbom:cyclonedx` or `sourcetype=spdx:json` with `component.name`, `component.version`; `index=pam` `sourcetype=cyberark:session` (user, target_host, duration_min) via Splunk Add-on for CyberArk ([Splunkbase 2891](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2891)).
app: Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928); optional Splunk SOAR (Splunkbase 5165) for supplier playbooks.
spl:
index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-30d NOT (STATUS=Fixed OR lower(STATUS)="fixed")
| eval asset=lower(trim(coalesce(HOSTNAME, host, fqdn)))
| lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS asset OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier jump_host
| where supplier_tier IN ("critical","tier1")
| eval severity_score=case(Severity="5",5,Severity="4",4,match(lower(coalesce(severity,"")),"critical"),5,match(lower(coalesce(severity,"")),"high"),4,0)
| where severity_score>=4
| stats dc(QID) as open_findings dc(CVE_ID) as distinct_cves dc(asset) as impacted_assets by supplier_name supplier_tier
| where open_findings>5 OR impacted_assets>8
| sort - open_findings
| table supplier_name supplier_tier open_findings distinct_cves impacted_assets
description: Correlates Qualys host findings that are still open and high severity against a `critical_suppliers.csv` inventory so only tier-one vendor estates with concentrated plugin counts appear, preparing companion SBOM and CyberArk panels.
value: Board packs gain a single narrative—"which indispensable supplier has the most serious unfixed exposures right now"—without three separate meetings with procurement, SecOps, and engineering, and it plugs directly into Art.21(3)/22 governance discussions about dependency management and incident cooperation.
implementation: (1) Ingest Qualys host detections into `index=vuln` with TA 2964 modular inputs hitting Qualys API **/api/3.0/fo/asset/host/vm/detection**. (2) Maintain `critical_suppliers.csv` under change control; columns must include managed_asset (FQDN lower-case) and supplier_tier. (3) Schedule this search daily; clone a second search for `index=sbom` CycloneDX joins on `component.version`. (4) Add CyberArk panel: `index=pam` stats on `target_host` where `jump_host` matches lookup. (5) Export composite PDF weekly to `index=audit_evidence`. (6) Optional: open ES (263) notable event when `open_findings>25`.
visualization: Supplier risk table, SBOM component treemap (optional panel), CyberArk vendor duration heatmap, ES risk index line chart.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t dc(Vulnerabilities.signature) as open_findings dc(Vulnerabilities.cve) as distinct_cves dc(Vulnerabilities.dest) as impacted_assets from datamodel=Vulnerabilities where Vulnerabilities.severity IN ("critical","high") by Vulnerabilities.vendor_product | rename Vulnerabilities.* as * | lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS dest OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier | where supplier_tier IN ("critical","tier1") | stats sum(open_findings) as open_findings sum(distinct_cves) as distinct_cves sum(impacted_assets) as impacted_assets by supplier_name supplier_tier | sort - open_findings
dataModelAcceleration: Recommended where ES Alerts datamodel is accelerated for executive rollups; otherwise rely on raw `index=vuln` searches scoped to 30 days.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Qualys STATUS field inconsistent after scanner upgrade** — Distinguish: uppercase `FIXED` vs `Fixed`. Suppress: normalize with `eval st=lower(STATUS)` then `where st!="fixed"`.
• **Shared scanning appliance shows duplicate QIDs** — Distinguish: same CVE listed per interface. Suppress: `dedup QID asset` before `stats`.
• **SBOM duplicates dev artifacts** — Distinguish: test images inflate component counts. Suppress: lookup `sbom_scope_exceptions.csv` marking `environment=prod_only`.
• **CyberArk sessions during approved vendor cutover** — Distinguish: CHG record exists in ServiceNow. Suppress: time-bound exception register `supplier_access_window.csv`.
• **critical_suppliers.csv stale after divestiture** — Distinguish: assets still flagged tier1 though business sold division. Suppress: nightly sync from ServiceNow CMDB via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow REST input filtered with `u_active=true`.
equipmentModels: CyberArk Privileged Access Security (PAM session logs for vendor jump-host monitoring)
Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964, host detection vulnerability data)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, supplier contract and assessment data)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (HEC for SBOM ingestion from CI/CD pipelines)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Assets & Identities for supplier tagging)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `vuln` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `critical_suppliers.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('critical_suppliers.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(d)`. The control objective is: "Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(d) using NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `vuln` under `qualys:hostdetection`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


7 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest Qualys host detections into `index=vuln` with TA 2964 modular inputs hitting Qualys API **/api/3.0/fo/asset/host/vm/detection**. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** Maintain `critical_suppliers.csv` under change control; columns must include managed_asset (FQDN lower-case) and supplier_tier. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule this search daily; clone a second search for `index=sbom` CycloneDX joins on `component.version`. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Add CyberArk panel: `index=pam` stats on `target_host` where `jump_host` matches lookup. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Export composite PDF weekly to `index=audit_evidence`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 6:** Optional: open ES. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 7:** notable event when `open_findings>25`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and vendor access monitoring," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `critical_suppliers.csv` — the vendor/third-party register tracking approved service providers. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`supplier_name supplier_tier jump_host`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `managed_asset`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `critical_suppliers.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval managed_asset = lower(managed_asset)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 30 days.

Numeric threshold `severity_score >= 4`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Vulnerabilities` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t dc(Vulnerabilities.signature) as open_findings dc(Vulnerabilities.cve) as distinct_cves dc(Vulnerabilities.dest) as impacted_assets from datamodel=Vulnerabilities where Vulnerabilities.severity IN ("critical","high") by Vulnerabilities.vendor_product | rename Vulnerabilities.* as * | lookup critical_suppliers.csv managed_asset AS dest OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier | where supplier_tier IN ("critical","tier1") | stats sum(open_findings) as open_findings sum(distinct_cves) as distinct_cves sum(impacted_assets) as impacted_assets by supplier_name supplier_tier | sort - open_findings
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Vulnerabilities` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.50: NIS2 Art.21/22 — Critical supplier risk, SBOM exposure and v]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.50,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `supplier_name`, `supplier_tier`, `open_findings`, `distinct_cves`, `impacted_assets`. Run:

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table supplier_name, supplier_tier, open_findings, distinct_cves, impacted_assets
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `critical_suppliers.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup critical_suppliers.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `severity_score`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(severity_score) as p50 perc90(severity_score) as p90 perc99(severity_score) as p99 max(severity_score) as hi
```

The current threshold `4` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.50: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Supplier Risk Table (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Sbom Component Treemap (`optional panel`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Cyberark Vendor Duration Heatmap (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Es Risk Index Line Chart (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We compare the list of suppliers you cannot afford to lose with the latest serious security holes and the time outside technicians spend inside your systems, so leaders see one clear picture before they sign off on risk.
